agent1_snippets.content: If the pancreatic adenocarcinoma is considered locally advanced, then by definition, it is unresectable. Neoadjuvant treatment with chemotherapy and/or radiation is typically preferred in this situation.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Radiation Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: There is a role for radiation therapy in combination with chemotherapy to treat locally advanced pancreatic cancer. Radiation therapy was originally used to alleviate pain, but its use is currently becoming more widespread to shrink tumors and increase survival.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Surgical Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: The neoadjuvant first approach in resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma is implemented more and more frequently at high-volume centers nationwide and internationally. The rationale behind the neoadjuvant first approach is that the patient is in the best shape to receive chemotherapy and at the best odds of completing chemotherapy for 4 to 6 months. In addition, tissue is thought to be well-oxygenated, having not undergone a large procedure such as the Whipple. Many patients do not complete or even start adjuvant chemotherapy after surgical resection, thereby decreasing their survival. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: If the adenocarcinoma of the pancreas is located in the body or tail of the pancreas, then the surgical procedure of choice is distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy. If there is extensive involvement of the celiac artery and splenic artery, a modified Appleby procedure can be performed if it meets the criteria. [11] Minimally invasive approaches to Whipple and distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy have been investigated with similar outcomes in survival, morbidity, and mortality. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Even though the majority of the pancreatic islet cell cancers are indolent, an aggressive approach is preferred to the “wait-and-see” approach when dealing with them. The aggressive approach includes taking out the primary and metastatic tumors. However, the implementation of the aggressive approach requires a team comprised of multiple specialties to deal with pre and post-surgical management. [20] The aggressive therapy for PNETs involves surgery, locoregional therapy followed by systemic therapy, and complication control. [21]

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Pancreatic cancer refers to the carcinoma arising from the pancreatic duct cells, pancreatic ductal carcinoma, and is the fourth leading cause of cancer deaths in the United States. The 5-year survival rate in the United States ranges from 5% to 15%. The overall survival rate is only 6%. Surgical resection is the only current option for a cure, but only 20% of pancreatic cancer is surgically resectable at the time of diagnosis. [1] [2] [3] [4] Close cooperation among various specialties, including surgeons, oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, and radiologists, is extremely important for the chance of survival in patients with resectable disease and borderline resectable disease.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: The diagnosis and management of pancreatic cancer is with an interprofessional team that consists of an oncologist, general surgeon, radiologist, pain specialist, gastroenterologist, palliative care nurse, and an internist. By far, the majority of cases of pancreatic cancer are advanced at the time of presentation, and these patients have a very short life expectancy. Palliative care and a pain specialist should be involved in the care of these patients. Coordination of education of the family by the palliative nurse or provider is key because survival is severely reduced in most patients. The surgery for pancreatic cancer is complex and technically demanding. Even those who undergo successful resection may develop serious life-threatening complications which may alter the quality of life. [17] [Level 5]

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Most patients have residual disease even after surgical and locoregional therapy; they require systemic therapy to deal with residual tumors. Functioning PNETs like VIPoma and glucagonoma are effectively managed with somatostatin analogs at this stage. Octreotide, lanreotide, and pasireotide are somatostatin analogs that are being used for this purpose. The role of somatostatin analogs in the management of nonfunctioning pancreatic tumors is controversial. [25]

agent1_snippets.content: The patients with recurrent, advanced, or metastatic pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors can often be poor candidates for surgical resection. This makes medical therapy the next best option for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Medical therapy can be either symptom directed or for controlling the growth of the tumor.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Prognosis
agent1_snippets.content: The prognosis for pancreatic adenocarcinoma remains poor despite advances in cancer therapy. The 5-year survival rate is approximately 20%. Prognosis after 1 year of diagnosis is dismal, with 90% of patients dying at 1 year despite surgery. However, palliative surgery can be of benefit.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: The secretion of a wide range of hormones is inhibited by somatostatin. Somatostatin analogs, like lanreotide and octreotide, have similar effects. Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors express somatostatin receptors, especially somatostatin receptor type 2. These somatostatin analogs decrease the secretion of hormones by binding to somatostatin receptors in the tumor. As far as the antiproliferative efficacy of somatostatin analogs is concerned, the CLARINET trial confirmed it. The results showed a significant progression-free survival in the long term when lanreotide was used in patients with well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. However, there was no significant increase in overall survival among the patients treated with lanreotide. [29]

agent1_snippets.title: Erlotinib -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Pancreatic cancer: The FDA approved erlotinib as a treatment for pancreatic cancer in 2005. Many human pancreatic cancers exhibit the EGFR mutation, and patients with this mutation often have a poor prognosis. Historically, gemcitabine monotherapy was the standard treatment for advanced pancreatic cancer. However, recent studies have shown promising results with combinations of gemcitabine and other anti-cancer agents, including erlotinib. The combination of erlotinib and gemcitabine has demonstrated superior benefits compared to gemcitabine monotherapy for patients with locally advanced or metastatic pancreatic cancer. Consequently, erlotinib, in combination with gemcitabine, is indicated as the first-line treatment for patients with locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic pancreatic cancer. Erlotinib has not been studied as a monotherapy for pancreatic cancer. [4]

The term cancer of pancreas, in turn, corresponds to the Arabic phrase “سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس,” which denotes a malignant tumor of the pancreas, typically arising in the head region. This alignment of terminology clarifies that carcinoma of the pancreatic head is a subset of pancreatic cancer and that the Arabic designation refers to the same disease entity, facilitating consistent understanding across languages and clinical contexts.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1033
agent2_snippets.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) and is directly mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas. Cancer of pancreas is classified as a metastatic cancer located in the head of the pancreas (سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس). Likewise, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is described as a malignant tumor (الورم السرطاني) whose associated morphology is that of metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي). These interconnections show that both the primary pancreatic carcinoma in the head and the metastatic carcinoma involving head‑and‑neck lymph nodes represent forms of malignant, often metastatic, neoplasms, underscoring the need to recognize their shared pathology for accurate diagnosis and effective patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
agent2_snippets.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

agent2_snippets.content: Tumor cells, benign are classified within the broader group “cancer; other and unspecified primary” but are regarded as a non‑finding of disease, meaning that the presence of benign tumor cells does not indicate the presence of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). In contrast, Tumor cells, malignant are also placed in the “cancer; other and unspecified primary” category, yet they are identified as an abnormal cell of disease and are specifically associated with السرطان البنكرياسي, reflecting that malignant tumor cells constitute the pathological hallmark of pancreatic cancer. Thus, while both benign and malignant tumor cells belong to the same general cancer classification, benign cells are not considered disease‑defining, whereas malignant cells represent the disease‑causing abnormal cellular component of pancreatic cancer.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.
